step1.outline:
[welcome-to-the-school-ye] Welcome to the School Year: Imagine Montessori School Valencia welcomes all families, especially new ones, to the 2024/25 school year. This handbook offers a guide to the school's general operation. The welcome is from Executive Director Mariano Pontón.
[index] Index: The index covers Imagine Montessori School Valencia's structure, including Pedagogical Team and Leadership. It details Timetables, School Hours, Punctuality, Drop-Off/Pick-Up, Rainy Days, Early Bird/Afternoon Club, and Authorisation for students to leave alone (school/bus). Communication, Pedagogical Meetings, and Curriculum (Early Years, Primary 6-12) are outlined, alongside the English language commitment and Psycho Pedagogical support. Key policies address Nutrition (healthy eating, food characteristics), Health Standards (Medications, Illnesses, Vaccination Status), Behavior, Dress Code, Nappies/Accidents, Birthday Celebrations (Early Years Community, Children's House), School's Materials, Toys, Balls, Lost and Found, Outings, and School Bus. Financial matters like Payment Policy and Financial Aid are included, plus a comparison of Spanish, British, and Montessori education systems.
[1] 1. Our School: Imagine Montessori School Valencia is a private, co-educational, secular school integrating Montessori (all levels) with the UK National Curriculum (DfES standards), recognized by UK DfES and the Spanish Ministry. It offers a creative, hands-on approach with English-speaking teachers.

For pedagogical or personal matters, contact your child's Guide. Primary classrooms (e.g., Daisy, Oak) have Montessori Guides & Assistants; Secondary (Y7-Y13) offers iGCSE/A-Levels with Tutors (e.g., Garbí) & subject teachers.

For non-teaching queries, use Phidias. Key contacts include Mariano (Executive Director), Sheila/Lindsay (Head Teachers for pedagogical/psychologist topics), Sandra (Head of School Operations for administration/Enrichment Program), and María Luisa/Madhu (Secretaries for absences, bus service, medication admin, Imagine Shop, lost & found, authorized collectors).

Crucially, use the Phidias Process for medication administration, the Google Form for bus changes, and the Imagine Shop for purchases. Avoid direct messages to administration.
  [1.1] 1.1 Imagine Montessori School Valencia: Imagine Montessori School Valencia is a private, co-educational, secular school. It integrates the Montessori curriculum (all levels) with the National Curriculum of England and Wales, adhering to UK DfES standards. Recognized by both the UK Department for Education (DfES) and the Spanish Ministry of Education, it offers a creative, hands-on approach. English-speaking teachers with international experience foster students' psychological, physical, and emotional development, curiosity, autonomy, creativity, and community belonging. This handbook guides families on school operations.
  [1.2] 1.2 Pedagogical Team: For pedagogical content and personal matters, contact your child's Guide. The teaching team is divided into Primary and Secondary. Primary classrooms (e.g., Daisy, Lavender, Pine, Oak) are staffed by Montessori Guides (Primary Montessori Guides for older primary), Assistants, and Support Assistants. Specialists provide instruction in Music, ICT/ESL, Physical Education, Spanish/Valencian, and Art. Secondary (Y7-Y13, including iGCSE and A-Levels) features Tutors for each 'Aula' (e.g., Garbí, Teíde) and dedicated subject teachers for English, Maths, Science, Spanish, Drama, Business, etc. The school also hosts visitors from universities and Montessori training centres for class observations.
  [1.3] 1.3 Leadership and Administration: For non-teaching staff queries, use Phidias. Mariano (Executive Director) handles school satisfaction/policies. Sheila/Lindsay (Head Teachers) cover pedagogical/psychologist topics. Sandra (Head of School Operations) manages administration/Enrichment Program. María Luisa (Admin) for payments. María Luisa/Madhu (Secretaries) handle absences, bus service, medication admin, Imagine Shop, lost & found, authorized collectors. Hernan assists with admissions/bus. Crucially, use the Phidias Process for medication admin, bus changes (Google Form), and Imagine Shop. Avoid direct messages to administration.
[2] 2. Timetables and Other Logistical Considerations: Imagine offers flexible, age-specific timetables: Early Bird (8-9h), Morning (9-12h), Reduced Day (9-15h), and Full Day (9-16:40h). Options vary by age group (Infant Community, Children's House, Primary/Secondary). Timetable changes require Guide consultation and Phidias confirmation.

School is open 8:00-17:30. Student arrival is 8:45-9:00, departure 16:40-16:55. A €5 late arrival fee applies from the 5th instance. Early pick-ups are strictly at 12:00, 14:30, or 16:00 at Reception. Late pick-up fees are €5 (until 17:15), €10 (until 17:30), and €20 (until 17:45). Notify absences or changes via Phidias message to admin/Guide.

For rainy days, students need a raincoat and wellies; umbrellas are not recommended. Arrival/pick-up logistics vary: Infant Community uses Calle Melissa; others use the parking lot door. Park in the school lot.

"Early-Bird" (8:00-8:45) and "Afternoon Club" (16:40-18:00) childcare is available, charged on your "monthly invoice". To authorise others to pick up or for students to leave alone, update the "Tracking" section in Phidias.
  [2.1] 2.1 Timetables: Imagine offers flexible, age-specific timetables: Early Bird (8h-9h), Morning Session (9h-12h), Reduced Day (9h-15h), and Full Day (9h-16:40h). Infant community can choose any option. Children's House (Nursery) offers Early Bird, Reduced Day, or Full Day. Children's House (Reception & Year 1) has Early Bird or Full Day. Primary and Secondary are Full Day only. To request a timetable change after the school year begins, families must first consult their Guide. If agreed, a confirmation message must be sent via Phidias to both the class Guide and school administration.
  [2.2] 2.2 School Hours, Punctuality Guidelines and Drop-Off and Pick-Up: School is open 8:00 a.m. - 5:30 p.m. Student arrival is 8:45 a.m. - 9:00 a.m., departure 4:40 p.m. - 4:55 p.m. Punctuality is vital; late arrivals disrupt learning. A €5 fee applies from the 5th late arrival. Early pick-ups are strictly at 12:00 p.m., 2:30 p.m., or 4:00 p.m. at Reception; arrive 5 mins prior. Notify absences or changes in arrival/departure (e.g., medical appointments) via Phidias message to admin/Guide or by calling from 8:45 am for unforeseen circumstances. Late pick-ups after 4:55 p.m. incur a "late pick-up fee": €5 (until 5:15 p.m.), €10 (until 5:30 p.m.), €20 (until 5:45 p.m.).
  [2.3] 2.3 Rainy Days: Rainy day rules: Students need a raincoat and wellies. Umbrellas are not recommended for school entry due to safety. Parents will get a notice about pick-up logistics during rain.
  [2.4] Arrival and Pick-up Logistics: For arrival, all students/parents (except Infant Community) use the parking lot door to the main lobby. Infant Community uses their Calle Melissa/roundabout entrance. Park in the school lot; avoid the Health Centre on Calle Melissa to prevent fines.

Pick-up: Infant Community at their Calle Melissa entrance. Children's House at their playground (Calle Melissa), called one by one. Primary/Secondary at the main door (parking lot side), called one by one. Siblings exit via the youngest child's door (unless parents instruct).
  [2.6] 2.6 Early Bird and Afternoon Club: "Early-Bird" (8:00-8:45 am) and "Afternoon Club" (4:40-6:00 pm) offer flexible, term-time childcare. Both can be used regularly or "one-off". No prior booking is needed for Early-Bird; attendance is recorded for both. Charges appear on your "monthly invoice". Early-Bird costs won't exceed the monthly service fee. For Afternoon Club, if daily rates exceed the monthly fee, the monthly rate applies. This supports "work-life balance" in a "safe, familiar environment".
  [2.7] 2.7 Authorisation for Students to Leave or Be Picked Up Alone (from the school or the bus): Student pick-up authorization defaults to parents, guardians, and legal representatives listed in Phidias records. For others to pick up, or for students to leave alone from school or the bus, parents must complete the "Tracking" section in Phidias. The "How to Authorise Student Pick-Up in Phidias" video guide explains this specific process.
[3] 3. Communication Between Families and the School: Imagine prioritizes swift, dynamic communication for student well-being. Administrative matters are in Spanish/English. Pedagogical matters are in English with a Spanish translation link, allowing teachers to focus on students.

**Phidias** is our primary tool for admin/pedagogical team to families. Access documents (menu, calendar, photos), payments, "pick-up authorisation," and the "Imagine Shop." It's also used for secondary grading/reporting.

**Transparent Classroom** (Infant Community, Children's House, Primary) is a **one-way** pedagogical tool for teachers to share observations and weekly activities.

Other communication channels:
*   **Individual Meetings**: End of 1st/3rd terms. Guides provide progress reports (via TC/Phidias) before meetings.
*   **Newsletter**: Periodic updates on school events.
*   **Daily Communication**: Infant Community/Casa de Niños Guides are available at drop-off/pick-up. Primary/Secondary communication with Guides is via Phidias.
*   **Tutorials**: Guides have weekly hou
[4] 4. Pedagogical Meetings: "Pedagogical Meetings" are held quarterly to align families and school on educational guidelines. We recommend at least one family member attends. Sessions are offered both in-person (more enriching for group dynamics) and online for convenience, with advance notice. Separate language sessions ensure inclusion. These meetings help families understand our pedagogical approach and get involved.
[5] 5. Our Curriculum: Our curriculum integrates the British curriculum with Montessori philosophy, promoting independence and a positive attitude towards learning. Classrooms feature accessible, sensory, hands-on Montessori materials for self-directed practice. Qualified teachers adhere to DfES guidelines.

Early Years (EYFS) focuses on 7 areas: Personal, Social, and Emotional Development (PSED), Physical Development (PD), Communication and Language (C&L), Literacy, Mathematics, Understanding the World (UW), and Expressive Arts and Design (EAD). These are addressed via the Montessori Work Cycle in individualized, mixed-age settings, encompassing 5 Montessori areas: Sensory, Cultural, Practical Life, Language, and Math.

Primary (6-12) follows National Curriculum standards within Montessori work cycles, utilizing mixed-age groups and the "Five Great Lessons" (Universe, Life, Humanity, Math, Language) as a framework for subjects. Montessori materials teach grammar, math operations, and geometry, leading to abstraction. A "Creative Lab" enhances STEM.
  [5.1] 5.1 First Years – Early Years: Our Early Years Foundation Stage (EYFS) and Primary Department provides a holistic education, fostering self-confidence, emotional, personal, and social development, independent learning, and enjoyment. The Infant Community focuses on three core EYFS areas: Personal, Social, and Emotional Development (PSED), Physical Development (PD), and Communication and Language (C&L). Once confident, students progress to Specific Areas: Literacy, Mathematics, Understanding the World (UW), and Expressive Arts and Design (EAD).

These 7 EYFS areas are addressed via the Montessori Work Cycle, integrated themes, and clubs (e.g., yoga, gardening, music). We champion individualized learning, working at each child's pace in mixed-age classrooms to promote cooperation. Children are assessed across the 7 EYFS areas, aligning with our 5 Montessori learning areas:
*   **Sensory:** Refines senses, classifies objects, prepares for language/math.
*   **Cultural:** Explores botany, science, art, geography, history with hands-on experiences.
*   **Practical Life:** Teaches daily tasks like serving lunch, dressing, and courtesy.
*   **Language:** Develops auditory discrimination, phonetics (sandpaper letters), reading (Montessori materials), writing (movable a
  [5.2] 5.2 Primary (6-12): My child, aged 6-12, will be in Primary, following National Curriculum standards within Montessori work cycles. Their conscious mind develops, fostering interest in reasons and group work. Mixed-age classrooms support social skills. The curriculum uses five "Great Lessons" (Universe, Life, Humanity, Math, Language) as a framework for subjects like science, history, and math, deepening understanding yearly. Skills like grammar, math operations, and geometry are taught with Montessori materials, leading to abstraction. A "Creative Lab" enhances STEM through hands-on projects, promoting deep understanding and problem-solving.
[6] 6.   [7.1] Healthy Eating and Educational Approach to Food: Imagine Montessori School Valencia offers nutritionally balanced, daily scratch-made meals using organic, seasonal, local ingredients. We avoid processed foods (added fats/sugars), artificial colorings/preservatives, and keep salt low. Filtered water is available daily. Menus adapt to certified medical needs (gastroenterologist/allergist). Students eat together in classrooms, setting tables with ceramic plates/metal cutlery. Our Montessori-aligned gardening program teaches food origin, preparation, and waste management (compost, recycling), incorporating student-grown produce into lunch, fostering understanding of food-land links and seasonal cycles.
  [7.4] 7.4 Practical Matters: Students must bring a named toothbrush and toothpaste on day 1. Menus are shared monthly via Phidias. Notify administration before 09:30 for occasional soft diets or late arrivals needing special/adapted menus. Late adapted menu requests without prior notice cannot be guaranteed. A light snack is offered at 16:00; families should provide an additional snack for pickup.

Parents provide a blanket when weather requires it. Costs are detailed in the fee document, found in the "Centre Documents" section on Phidias.
[9] 9. Health Standards: Parents must not send sick children to school. Children must stay home for 24 hours after the last fever (37.5ºC/99.5ºF), vomiting, or diarrhea. Other reasons to stay home include strep throat (24h on antibiotics), cold/flu, head lice (until treated), rash, or conjunctivitis (24h on antibiotics). For medication, complete the Phidias tracking form and provide signed authorization to the office/bus monitor. Only oral medications with a medical prescription and written family authorization are administered. Meds needed every 8+ hours should be given at home. If a child falls ill at school, immediate pickup is required (parent, then emergency contacts, or ambulance if critical).

Enrolling signifies a conscious parental commitment to support English education. This includes encouraging English reading, prioritizing English speech over Spanish, using English music for listening/vocabulary, watching original English films/cartoons, and demonstrating interest by speaking or learning English.

The Imagine sports shirt is mandatory for PE classes and excursions for safety/logistics. ALL clothing (socks, shoes, blankets, etc.) must be labelled with the student's name for easy identification; hanging items need long labels for self-hanging. Early Years clothing should be comfortable, practical, and easy to remove (e.g., elastic waistbands) to foster independence. Shoes should be easy for students to manage themselves.
[13] 13. Spare Clothing and Required Equipment: Families must provide labelled spare clothing: 2 sets for 2-3 year olds, 1 set for 4+. Replace if used. Students need closed-toe shoes/slippers for free play (if wearing sandals) and soft-soled shoes/indoor slippers for classroom/work patio. Contracting the service occurs at academic year-end. For late requests, contact `admin@imaginemontessori.es` (subject to space).

Any changes (child not taking bus, exceptional stop change) must be communicated via a specific form *before 2:30 p.m.* No changes are accepted after this time or by phone/email. If picking up your child after 2:30 p.m., notify the secretary at school between 2:30 p.m. and 4:30 p.m. The bus departs at 4:50 p.m.

Individual and Group Photographs: Imagine's policy requires all families to consent to sharing photos/videos of their children, taken by guides in shared spaces/teams to document progress, with other school families. For external media (press, website, social media), specific authorization is required; fewer images may be taken without it. The Annual Yearbook features professional individual/group photos of all children. Digital copies are provided at an additional cost, with advance notice. This digital format supports family preferences for various uses and aligns with sustainability principles.

Bus/activities are charged separately monthly. Proformas are sent by the 25th, direct debits by the 1st. Maria Luisa handles payment queries. Outings are charged monthly (Phidias authorization); timely notice is required for refunds. Enrichment is quarterly (first late Sept/early Oct). Next course enrollment is charged monthly Sept-June; refunded if non-continuity is communicated before Feb 1, otherwise charged until June. Returned direct debits incur a 2.5% bank commission.

[Section 2.1: 2.1 Timetables]
Since each child is unique, the timetables at Imagine have been designed to meet the needs of both the children and their families, with a schedule that varies according to the child’s age.

|  | 8h – 9h Early Bird | 9h – 12h Morning Session | 9h – 15h Reduced Day | 9h – 16:40h Full Day |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Infant community** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Children’s House (Nursery)** | ✓ | x | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Children’s House (Reception & Year 1)** | ✓ | x | x | ✓ |
| **Primary** | x | x | x |  ✓ |
| **Secondary** | x | x | x |  ✓ |

Any changes to the timetables once the school year has begun (within the options shown above) will follow the process outlined below:

1.  The family must consult with their Guide about the timetable change.
2.  If a timetable change is agreed upon with the Guide, **the family must send a message via Phidias to both their class Guide and the school’s administration.** This email will serve as confirmation of the timetable change.

---

[Section 2.6: 2.6 Early Bird and Afternoon Club]
Our **Early-Bird** service is available from **8:00 am to 8:45 am**. This service is available on all school days throughout the term and can be used either regularly or on a one-off basis. No prior notice or booking is required; attendance will be confirmed with the responsible person. The charge for this service will be included with the next monthly invoice and will not exceed the cost of the monthly service.

We are also introducing a new service to support families with work-life balance: the **Afternoon Club**, available from **4:40 pm to 6:00 pm**. This is a relaxed and creative space for children to stay at school after the regular day, engaging in playful and calm activities in a safe, familiar environment. This allows families who need extra time to pick up their children later, knowing they are well cared for.

Like Early-Bird, this service can be used either on a **regular basis** or **occasionally as needed**. Attendance will be recorded, and charges will appear on the monthly invoice. If the accumulated daily rate exceeds the monthly fee, the monthly rate will apply.

Communication, Pedagogical Meetings, and Curriculum (Early Years, Primary 6-12) are outlined, alongside the English language commitment and Psycho Pedagogical support. Key policies address Nutrition (healthy eating, food characteristics), Health Standards (Medications, Illnesses, Vaccination Status), Behavior, Dress Code, Nappies/Accidents, Birthday Celebrations (Early Years Community, Children's House), School's Materials, Toys, Balls, Lost and Found, Outings, and School Bus. Financial matters like Payment Policy and Financial Aid are included, plus a comparison of Spanish, British, and Montessori education systems.

Our School: Imagine Montessori School Valencia is a private, co-educational, secular school integrating Montessori (all levels) with the UK National Curriculum (DfES standards), recognized by UK DfES and the Spanish Ministry. It offers a creative, hands-on approach with English-speaking teachers.

For pedagogical or personal matters, contact your child's Guide. Primary classrooms (e.g., Daisy, Oak) have Montessori Guides & Assistants; Secondary (Y7-Y13) offers iGCSE/A-Levels with Tutors (e.g., Garbí) & subject teachers.

For non-teaching queries, use Phidias. Key contacts include Mariano (Executive Director), Sheila/Lindsay (Head Teachers for pedagogical/psychologist topics), Sandra (Head of School Operations for administration/Enrichment Program), and María Luisa/Madhu (Secretaries for absences, bus service, medication admin, Imagine Shop, lost & found, authorized collectors).

  [1.1] 1.1 Imagine Montessori School Valencia: Imagine Montessori School Valencia is a private, co-educational, secular school. Recognized by both the UK Department for Education (DfES) and the Spanish Ministry of Education, it offers a creative, hands-on approach. English-speaking teachers with international experience foster students' psychological, physical, and emotional development, curiosity, autonomy, creativity, and community belonging. This handbook guides families on school operations.

Monthly menus are shared via Phidias. Notify administration before 09:30 for occasional soft diets or late adapted menu requests; late requests aren't guaranteed. A light snack is offered at 16:00; families provide an additional snack for pickup.
  [7.1] Healthy Eating and Educational Approach to Food: Imagine Montessori School Valencia offers nutritionally balanced, daily scratch-made meals using organic, seasonal, local ingredients. We avoid processed foods (added fats/sugars), artificial colorings/preservatives, and keep salt low. Filtered water is available daily. Menus adapt to certified medical needs (gastroenterologist/allergist). Students eat together in classrooms, setting tables with ceramic plates/metal cutlery. Our Montessori-aligned gardening program teaches food origin, preparation, and waste management (compost, recycling), incorporating student-grown produce into lunch, fostering understanding of food-land links and seasonal cycles.
  [7.4] 7.4 Practical Matters: Students must bring a named toothbrush and toothpaste on day 1. Menus are shared monthly via Phidias. Notify administration before 09:30 for occasional soft diets or late arrivals needing special/adapted menus. Late adapted menu requests without prior notice cannot be guaranteed. A light snack is offered at 16:00; families should provide an additional snack for pickup.
[8] 8. Nap: Infant Community and Nursery (Casa de Niños) students have a rest period after lunch. The school provides a fitted sheet at the start of the year, including year-round laundry service. Parents provide a blanket when weather requires it. Costs are detailed in the fee document, found in the "Centre Documents" section on Phidias.
[9] 9. Health Standards: Parents must not send sick children to school. Children must stay home for 24 hours after the last fever (37.5ºC/99.5ºF), vomiting, or diarrhea. Other reasons to stay home include strep throat (24h on antibiotics), cold/flu, head lice (until treated), rash, or conjunctivitis (24h on antibiotics). For medication, complete the Phidias tracking form and provide signed authorization to the office/bus monitor. Only oral medications with a medical prescription and written family authorization are administered. Meds needed every 8+ hours should be given at home. If a child falls ill at school, immediate pickup is required (parent, then emergency contacts, or ambulance if critical).

Families must provide a medical certificate confirming adherence to the Valencian Community's vaccination schedule. This is vital for "herd immunity" and protecting the "immunocompromised." Certificates are required during Imagine admission and subsequently at ages 4, 6, 12, and 14.
  [9.1] 9.1 Medications and Illnesses: Do not send sick children to school. They must stay home for a minimum of 24 hours after the last fever (37.5ºC/99.5ºF or higher), vomiting, or diarrhea. Other reasons to stay home include strep throat (24h on antibiotics), cold/flu, head lice (until treated), rash, or conjunctivitis (24h on antibiotics). For medication, complete the Phidias tracking form. Hand meds to the office/bus monitor with signed authorization. We only administer oral medications with a medical prescription and written family authorization. Meds needed every 8+ hours should be given at home. If your child falls ill at school, we will contact you for immediate pickup, then emergency contacts, or call an ambulance if critical.
  [9.2] 9.2 Vaccination Status: Families must provide a medical certificate confirming adherence to the Valencian Community's vaccination schedule. This is crucial for student and community health, supporting "herd immunity" to protect the "immunocompromised." Certificates are required during the Imagine admission process, and subsequently at ages 4, 6, 12, and 14.
[10] 10. Behavior: For student behavior incidents during school hours, Imagine uses a referral form. Families are informed within 24 hours if a form is completed. After three incidents, a meeting is arranged with the family, teacher, and educational psychology team for early intervention to improve the situation and foster a positive school-home connection, ensuring a happy, safe environment.
[11] 11. Our Commitment to the English Language: As a British school, Imagine Montessori School Valencia's language of instruction is English. Enrolling signifies a conscious parental commitment to support English education. This includes encouraging English reading, prioritizing English speech over Spanish, using English music for listening/vocabulary, watching original English films/cartoons, and demonstrating interest by speaking or learning English.
[12] 12. Dress Code: Imagine does not require school uniforms, promoting student uniqueness. Optional garments are available via Phidias in the Imagine Shop. The Imagine sports shirt is mandatory for PE classes and excursions for safety/logistics. ALL clothing (socks, shoes, blankets, etc.) must be labelled with the student's name for easy identification; hanging items need long labels for self-hanging. Early Years clothing should be comfortable, practical, and easy to remove (e.g., elastic waistbands) to foster independence. Shoes should be easy for students to manage themselves.
[13] 13. Spare Clothing and Required Equipment: Families must provide labelled spare clothing: 2 sets for 2-3 year olds, 1 set for 4+. Replace if used. Students need closed-toe shoes/slippers for free play (if wearing sandals) and soft-soled shoes/indoor slippers for classroom/work patio. A maximum coverage hat is required for sunny seasons to prevent heatstroke/sunburn. Apply sun protection mornings; school reapplies. Ages 3+ practice self-application (roll-on helps).
[14] 14. Nappies and Accidents: Families must provide nappies & wipes for students using them in the Early Years Community. Children's House students are expected to be toilet-trained. For accidents, staff assist students to clean/change using family-provided spare clothes. Soiled items go home at pick-up; families must provide a new set to ensure two complete sets of spare clothes are always at school. A general change of clothes is also requested for unforeseen circumstances.
[15] 15. Birthday Celebrations (Early Years Community and Children's House): Birthday celebrations in Early Years/Children's House focus on recognizing growth and family involvement. Parents are asked to send "guide photos" (birth + one per year). Students can leave an enduring item for the classroom, such as a decorated vase or a library book. Due to potential allergies, there is a strict "no-foods policy"; the school provides usual snacks. We do not distribute party favors or invitations. Primary and Secondary teachers may modify celebration types.
[16] 16. Enrichment Programme: Our "Enrichment Programme" is an innovative extracurricular activities program for children and teenagers. It fosters creativity, social skills, and overall development through fun and learning, supported by highly trained professionals. Parents can enroll their child via the dedicated online process.
[17] 17. School’s Materials: If your child accidentally takes school educational material, please return the item to reception promptly. This is crucial because each piece belongs to a set, and incomplete sets become unusable. Your quick action ensures all learning resources remain functional.
[18] 18. Toys: Toys are generally not permitted at school. The only exception is a comfort item for naptime, but only if recommended by the guides. For Early Years children, pacifiers are permitted solely during naptime, if necessary, due to hygiene reasons and to aid language development.
[19] 19. Balls: Students may bring balls. For bus riders, balls must be kept in a bag, not loose, to prevent accidents. The school is not responsible for lost balls, including those that end up in the ditch. Balls are strictly for outdoor use, such as the playground. Misuse inside the premises will result in confiscation.
[20] 20. Lost and Found: The Lost and Found is located in a CH playground storage area. To help families recover misplaced items, all unclaimed belongings will be displayed monthly in the playground, typically on the last Friday afternoon. The exact day and time will be announced in advance. Please note: unclaimed items without a child's name will be cleared after this display day and may be donated or discarded. We strongly recommend labeling all personal belongings with your child’s name for easy return.
[21] 21. Outings: Outings complement learning, scheduled or ad-hoc, with potential additional costs communicated in advance. Children must participate as a group, starting and ending the day at school, using school-chosen transport for the full experience (e.g., Work Circle, behavioural rules). No personal booster seats are allowed on buses due to transport company policies. A specific consent form, signed via Phidias (not Secretary’s office), is mandatory for participation. Non-participating students attend school normally for same-day trips. Participation is ONLY with a signed consent form.
[22] 22. School Bus: The school bus service offers three routes, available for annual round-trip or one-way journeys. Routes may vary yearly, prioritizing continuity. This year, departure is 10 minutes later, but arrival times are unchanged due to 25/26 optimization.

Contracting the service occurs at academic year-end. For late requests, contact `admin@imaginemontessori.es` (subject to space).

Any changes (child not taking bus, exceptional stop change) must be communicated via a specific form *before 2:30 p.m.* No changes are accepted after this time or by phone/email. If picking up your child after 2:30 p.m., notify the secretary at school between 2:30 p.m. and 4:30 p.m. The bus departs at 4:50 p.m.
[23] 23. Updating Personal Information or Family Situation: Parents must update "contact information" or "important records" in Phidias immediately if they change, ensuring current data for emergencies. A video explains "How to edit your profile on Phidias." For changes in "family situation" (e.g., separation, divorce), inform your child's "guide" promptly so they can monitor any impact on the child.
[24] 24. Individual and Group Photographs: Imagine's policy requires all families to consent to sharing photos/videos of their children, taken by guides in shared spaces/teams to document progress, with other school families. For external media (press, website, social media), specific authorization is required; fewer images may be taken without it. The Annual Yearbook features professional individual/group photos of all children. Digital copies are provided at an additional cost, with advance notice. This digital format supports family preferences for various uses and aligns with sustainability principles.
[25] 25. Payment Policy and Financial Aid: Imagine's fees, updated annually, are paid monthly via direct debit. A flat monthly rate covers prorated costs, materials, and next course registration. Bus/activities are charged separately monthly. Proformas are sent by the 25th, direct debits by the 1st. Maria Luisa handles payment queries. Outings are charged monthly (Phidias authorization); timely notice is required for refunds. Enrichment is quarterly (first late Sept/early Oct). Next course enrollment is charged monthly Sept-June; refunded if non-continuity is communicated before Feb 1, otherwise charged until June. Returned direct debits incur a 2.5% bank commission.

Financial aid includes Valencia City Council (0-6 years) and Paterna City Council (0-3 years) School Vouchers, plus Sodexo nursery vouchers (Pluxe, UP Spain, Gourmet). Working mothers can claim a tax deduction via Form 233. Note: Lunch Aid, Generalitat Valenciana Childcare Voucher, and 0-3 Year Schooling Aid are NOT applicable due to Imagine's private, British curriculum status. Contact admin@imaginemontessori.es for financial aid questions.

Bus/activities are charged separately monthly. Proformas are sent by the 25th, direct debits issued by the 1st. Contact Maria Luisa for questions. Outings are charged in the month they occur; Phidias authorization is used. Refunds for outings require timely notice. Enrichment is charged quarterly (first one late Sept/early Oct). Enrollment for the next course is charged monthly Sept-June; refunded if non-continuity is communicated before Feb 1, otherwise charged until June. Returned direct debits incur a 2.5% bank commission.
  [25.2] 25.2 Financial Aid: Families at Imagine can apply for Valencia City Council (0-6 years, May-April) and Paterna City Council (0-3 years, early May) School Vouchers. Sodexo nursery vouchers (Pluxe, UP Spain, Gourmet) are accepted. Working mothers can claim a tax deduction for schooling expenses via Form 233. Important: Lunch Aid, Generalitat Valenciana Childcare Voucher, and 0-3 Year Schooling Aid are NOT applicable to Imagine due to its private status and British curriculum. Contact admin@imaginemontessori.es for questions.
[26] 26. Comparison of the Spanish Education System vs the British System vs Montessori: Parents can compare British, Spanish, & Montessori education stages. The British system progresses from Early Years (Pre-Nursery, Nursery, Reception) to Key Stages 1-5 (Years 1-13). Spanish follows Educación Infantil (1º/2º ciclo e.i.), Primaria (1º-6º), ESO (1º-4º), then Bachiller (1º-2º). Montessori includes Infant Community, Children’s House, Lower/Upper Primary, and specific KS equivalents for adolescents. Ages 2-18 are covered.
[27] 27. School Policies: School policies are shared with families on Phidias, specifically within the "School Documents" section.

[Section 2: 2. Timetables and Other Logistical Considerations]
## 2.1 Timetables

Since each child is unique, the timetables at Imagine have been designed to meet the needs of both the children and their families, with a schedule that varies according to the child’s age.

|  | 8h – 9h Early Bird | 9h – 12h Morning Session | 9h – 15h Reduced Day | 9h – 16:40h Full Day |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Infant community** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Children’s House (Nursery)** | ✓ | x | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Children’s House (Reception & Year 1)** | ✓ | x | x | ✓ |
| **Primary** | x | x | x |  ✓ |
| **Secondary** | x | x | x |  ✓ |

Any changes to the timetables once the school year has begun (within the options shown above) will follow the process outlined below:

1.  The family must consult with their Guide about the timetable change.
2.  If a timetable change is agreed upon with the Guide, **the family must send a message via Phidias to both their class Guide and the school’s administration.** This email will serve as confirmation of the timetable change.

## 2.2 School Hours, Punctuality Guidelines and Drop-Off and Pick-Up

**School Opening Hours:** 8:00 a.m. - 5:30 p.m.
**Reception Hours:** 8:45 a.m. - 1:00 p.m. and 2:30 p.m. - 4:55 p.m.
**Arrival Time:** 8:45 a.m. - 9:00 a.m.
**Departure Time:** 4:40 p.m. - 4:55 p.m.

**The Importance of Punctuality**
We encourage families to arrive before 9:00 a.m., as:

*   Guides personally greet each student between 8:45 a.m. and 9:00 a.m., creating a welcoming atmosphere that encourages students to begin their activities proactively.
*   Arrivals after 9:00 a.m. disrupt the flow, as students who arrived on time have already started their activities, and guides are engaged in presenting materials. This may cause a late-arriving student to feel disoriented or unsettled.
*   Punctuality fosters a focused and productive environment, supporting students in developing autonomy and a sense of responsibility, while building positive habits for their future growth.

**EARLY PICK-UPS** –

For pick-ups, we ask that families or legal guardians arrive 5 minutes before the designated pick-up time.

As pick-ups outside the set schedule disrupt class dynamics and affect students' workflow, the only available pick-up times are as follows:

*   12:00 p.m.
*   2:30 p.m.
*   4:00 p.m.

Early pick-ups will take place at Reception.

**ABSENCES OR CHANGES IN ARRIVAL OR DEPARTURE TIMES** – Please notify in advance any student absences to:

*   By sending a Phidias message to the administration and the student’s Guide or tutor.
*   By calling the school from 8:45 am in cases of unforeseen circumstances.

Similarly, any changes in school arrival/departure times (e.g., medical appointments) should be reported, so they can be taken into account when planning daily activities and/or student logistics.

**LATE ARRIVALS** – Students who arrive late significantly disrupt the learning environment and the education of other students. When students arrive late, they miss essential instructions given at the beginning of the day, which can significantly impact their performance, regardless of their academic ability. Additionally, students may feel uncomfortable entering the classroom when others are already settled.

Late arrivals also interrupt the entire class and the teaching staff, compromising the learning experience for everyone.

Starting from the fifth late arrival during the school year, **a fee of €5 per late arrival will be charged on a monthly basis.** Information about late arrivals is recorded on a form; if a high frequency of late arrivals is detected, the Guides will arrange a meeting with the family and the teaching team to assess how we can support the family.

**LATE PICK-UPS** – If, for any reason, families arrive for pick-up after 4:55 pm, a "late pick-up fee" will be charged on the corresponding monthly invoice. The fees according to the delay are as follows:

*   From 4:55 pm to 5:15 pm – 5 € per late pick-up
*   From 5:16 pm to 5:30 pm – 10 € per late pick-up
*   From 5:31 pm to 5:45 pm – 20 € per late pick-up

## 2.3 Rainy Days

*   On rainy days, it is recommended that students **bring a raincoat and wellies**.
*   Additionally, **we do not recommend the use of umbrellas** when entering the school for safety reasons.
*   In the event of rain, you will **receive a notice** informing you about how the pick-up logistics will be handled.

## Arrival and Pick-up Logistics

**All students and parents/guardians** (except for the Infant Community) will enter the school through the door facing the school parking lot and walk to the main lobby, where staff will welcome the students and guide them to their classrooms.

All families should park their vehicles in the school parking lot to avoid traffic congestion and adhere to parking restrictions in the area. Please note that the parking area at the Health Centre on Calle Melissa is designated for its doctors, nurses, staff, and patients, and you may receive a fine if you park there.

Students from the **Infant Community** will use their own entrance on Calle Melissa and the roundabout.

*   **Infant Community Students**: They will be picked up at the Infant Community entrance on Calle Melisa.
*   **Children’s House Students**: They will be picked up in the Children’s House playground, located on Calle Melissa. They will be called one by one in the order of arrival.
*   **Primary and Secondary Students**: They will exit through the main door, facing the school parking lot. They will be called one by one in the order of arrival.
*   **Siblings**: All siblings will exit together through the door of the youngest child (unless different instructions are provided by the parents).

## 2.6 Early Bird and Afternoon Club

Our **Early-Bird** service is available from **8:00 am to 8:45 am**. This service is available on all school days throughout the term and can be used either regularly or on a one-off basis. No prior notice or booking is required; attendance will be confirmed with the responsible person. The charge for this service will be included with the next monthly invoice and will not exceed the cost of the monthly service.

We are also introducing a new service to support families with work-life balance: the **Afternoon Club**, available from **4:40 pm to 6:00 pm**. This is a relaxed and creative space for children to stay at school after the regular day, engaging in playful and calm activities in a safe, familiar environment. This allows families who need extra time to pick up their children later, knowing they are well cared for.

Like Early-Bird, this service can be used either on a **regular basis** or **occasionally as needed**. Attendance will be recorded, and charges will appear on the monthly invoice. If the accumulated daily rate exceeds the monthly fee, the monthly rate will apply.

## 2.7 Authorisation for Students to Leave or Be Picked Up Alone (from the school or the bus)

Parents, guardians, and legal representatives who have properly completed their information in our Phidias records will be authorised to pick up the students.

If someone other than those listed will be picking up the students, or if you wish to authorise the students to leave on their own, you must complete the “Tracking” section in Phidias, as explained below:

[How to Authorise Student Pick-Up in Phidias](https://vimeo.com/1003094042/7c8aaad568)

---

[How to Authorise Student Pick-Up in Phidias](https://vimeo.com/1003094042/7c8aaad568)

[Section 3: 3. Communication Between Families and the School]
At Imagine, we believe that smooth communication between families and the school is crucial. We aim for this communication to be swift and dynamic, as we know it contributes to the well-being of the students and the peace of mind of the families. To facilitate this, we use several communication platforms such as Phidias and Transparent Classroom.

The language of communication will be:

1) Administrative and secretarial matters: in Spanish and in English.
2) Pedagogical matters: in English with a link to the Spanish translation. The rationale for providing a link to the Spanish translation of pedagogical emails in English is to assist Spanish-speaking families in understanding the content, while allowing the teaching staff to dedicate the maximum amount of their time to activities related to the children rather than translating texts.

**Phidias** is our communication tool between our pedagogical team or administration and our families. Through Phidias, families have access to folders with relevant documents from the school, such as the monthly menu, photos, school calendar, yearbooks, and more. In each user’s personal space, you can access payments, the “pick-up authorisation” process, and the “Imagine Shop” for purchasing school materials.

For secondary students, Phidias will also be used for the grading and reporting system.

**Transparent Classroom** is a pedagogical communication tool used in the Infant Community, Children’s House, and Primary sections where our pedagogical team will share observations about the students and other weekly activities. It is a **one-way** communication channel from the teaching staff to the families.

The flow of communication from the school to families will also include the following channels:

**Individual Meetings with Families**: These meetings take place at the end of the first and third terms. The Guide provides a summary of the student's progress to the family, along with a written report, which will be sent via Transparent Classroom or Phidias, depending on the stage, before the meeting so that families can address any questions during the meeting.

**Newsletter**: The purpose of this periodic newsletter is to share with families the most relevant events in the school community.

**Daily Communication**: Guides in the Infant Community and Casa de Niños are available at drop-off and pick-up times to discuss anything families wish to share. For Primary and Secondary, communication with Guides will be via Phidias.

**Tutorials**: Each Guide has designated weekly tutorial hours to address specific issues throughout the year. You can request these directly from your Guide via Phidias.

For any administrative matters, you can contact our administration team directly through Phidias.

  [2.2] 2.2 School Hours, Punctuality Guidelines and Drop-Off and Pick-Up: School is open 8:00 a.m. - 5:30 p.m. Student arrival is 8:45 a.m. - 9:00 a.m., departure 4:40 p.m. - 4:55 p.m. Punctuality is vital; late arrivals disrupt learning. A €5 fee applies from the 5th late arrival. Early pick-ups are strictly at 12:00 p.m., 2:30 p.m., or 4:00 p.m. at Reception; arrive 5 mins prior. Notify absences or changes in arrival/departure (e.g., medical appointments) via Phidias message to admin/Guide or by calling from 8:45 am for unforeseen circumstances. Late pick-ups after 4:55 p.m. incur a "late pick-up fee": €5 (until 5:15 p.m.), €10 (until 5:30 p.m.), €20 (until 5:45 p.m.).
  [2.3] 2.3 Rainy Days: Rainy day rules: Students need a raincoat and wellies. Umbrellas are not recommended for school entry due to safety. Parents will get a notice about pick-up logistics during rain.
  [2.4] Arrival and Pick-up Logistics: For arrival, all students/parents (except Infant Community) use the parking lot door to the main lobby. Infant Community uses their Calle Melissa/roundabout entrance. Park in the school lot; avoid the Health Centre on Calle Melissa to prevent fines.

Pick-up: Infant Community at their Calle Melissa entrance. Children's House at their playground (Calle Melissa), called one by one. Primary/Secondary at the main door (parking lot side), called one by one. Siblings exit via the youngest child's door (unless parents instruct).
  [2.6] 2.6 Early Bird and Afternoon Club: "Early-Bird" (8:00-8:45 am) and "Afternoon Club" (4:40-6:00 pm) offer flexible, term-time childcare. Both can be used regularly or "one-off". No prior booking is needed for Early-Bird; attendance is recorded for both. Charges appear on your "monthly invoice". Early-Bird costs won't exceed the monthly service fee. For Afternoon Club, if daily rates exceed the monthly fee, the monthly rate applies. This supports "work-life balance" in a "safe, familiar environment".
  [2.7] 2.7 Authorisation for Students to Leave or Be Picked Up Alone (from the school or the bus): Student pick-up authorization defaults to parents, guardians, and legal representatives listed in Phidias records. For others to pick up, or for students to leave alone from school or the bus, parents must complete the "Tracking" section in Phidias. The "How to Authorise Student Pick-Up in Phidias" video guide explains this specific process.
[3] 3. Communication Between Families and the School: Imagine prioritizes swift, dynamic communication for student well-being. Administrative matters are in Spanish/English. Pedagogical matters are in English with a Spanish translation link, allowing teachers to focus on students.

**Phidias** is our primary tool for admin/pedagogical team to families. Access documents (menu, calendar, photos), payments, "pick-up authorisation," and the "Imagine Shop." It's also used for secondary grading/reporting.

**Transparent Classroom** (Infant Community, Children's House, Primary) is a **one-way** pedagogical tool for teachers to share observations and weekly activities.

Other communication channels:
*   **Individual Meetings**: End of 1st/3rd terms. Guides provide progress reports (via TC/Phidias) before meetings.
*   **Newsletter**: Periodic updates on school events.
*   **Daily Communication**: Infant Community/Casa de Niños Guides are available at drop-off/pick-up. Primary/Secondary communication with Guides is via Phidias.
*   **Tutorials**: Guides have weekly hou
[4] 4. Pedagogical Meetings: "Pedagogical Meetings" are held quarterly to align families and school on educational guidelines. We recommend at least one family member attends. Sessions are offered both in-person (more enriching for group dynamics) and online for convenience, with advance notice. Separate language sessions ensure inclusion. These meetings help families understand our pedagogical approach and get involved.
[5] 5. Our Curriculum: Our curriculum integrates the British curriculum with Montessori philosophy, promoting independence and a positive attitude towards learning. Classrooms feature accessible, sensory, hands-on Montessori materials for self-directed practice. Qualified teachers adhere to DfES guidelines.

Early Years (EYFS) focuses on 7 areas: Personal, Social, and Emotional Development (PSED), Physical Development (PD), Communication and Language (C&L), Literacy, Mathematics, Understanding the World (UW), and Expressive Arts and Design (EAD). These are addressed via the Montessori Work Cycle in individualized, mixed-age settings, encompassing 5 Montessori areas: Sensory, Cultural, Practical Life, Language, and Math.

Primary (6-12) follows National Curriculum standards within Montessori work cycles, utilizing mixed-age groups and the "Five Great Lessons" (Universe, Life, Humanity, Math, Language) as a framework for subjects. Montessori materials teach grammar, math operations, and geometry, leading to abstraction. A "Creative Lab" enhances STEM.
  [5.1] 5.1 First Years – Early Years: Our Early Years Foundation Stage (EYFS) and Primary Department provides a holistic education, fostering self-confidence, emotional, personal, and social development, independent learning, and enjoyment. The Infant Community focuses on three core EYFS areas: Personal, Social, and Emotional Development (PSED), Physical Development (PD), and Communication and Language (C&L). Once confident, students progress to Specific Areas: Literacy, Mathematics, Understanding the World (UW), and Expressive Arts and Design (EAD).

These 7 EYFS areas are addressed via the Montessori Work Cycle, integrated themes, and clubs (e.g., yoga, gardening, music). We champion individualized learning, working at each child's pace in mixed-age classrooms to promote cooperation. Children are assessed across the 7 EYFS areas, aligning with our 5 Montessori learning areas:
*   **Sensory:** Refines senses, classifies objects, prepares for language/math.
*   **Cultural:** Explores botany, science, art, geography, history with hands-on experiences.
*   **Practical Life:** Teaches daily tasks like serving lunch, dressing, and courtesy.
*   **Language:** Develops auditory discrimination, phonetics (sandpaper letters), reading (Montessori materials), writing (movable a
  [5.2] 5.2 Primary (6-12): My child, aged 6-12, will be in Primary, following National Curriculum standards within Montessori work cycles. Their conscious mind develops, fostering interest in reasons and group work. Mixed-age classrooms support social skills. The curriculum uses five "Great Lessons" (Universe, Life, Humanity, Math, Language) as a framework for subjects like science, history, and math, deepening understanding yearly. Skills like grammar, math operations, and geometry are taught with Montessori materials, leading to abstraction. A "Creative Lab" enhances STEM through hands-on projects, promoting deep understanding and problem-solving.
[6] 6. Psycho Pedagogical and Career Guidance Department: Imagine's Psycho Pedagogical Guidance identifies special educational needs, advises families on external specialists for diagnosis, and coordinates school/home adjustments. It does not diagnose or provide therapy. Individualised support, if recommended, is an additional service in monthly fees. Career Guidance offers vocational counselling, info on universities/career pathways, and develops employability skills (CVs, interviews). It also facilitates talks and internship programmes, helping students make informed academic and professional choices.
[7] 7. Nutrition: Imagine Montessori School Valencia provides daily scratch-made, organic, seasonal, local meals, avoiding processed foods (added fats/sugars), artificial colorings/preservatives, and low salt. Filtered water is available daily. Menus adapt to certified medical needs (gastroenterologist/allergist). Students eat communally in classrooms, using ceramic plates/metal cutlery. A Montessori gardening program teaches food origin, prep, and waste management (compost, recycling), integrating student-grown produce. Parents must bring a named toothbrush and toothpaste on day 1. Monthly menus are shared via Phidias. Notify administration before 09:30 for occasional soft diets or late adapted menu requests; late requests aren't guaranteed. A light snack is offered at 16:00; families provide an additional snack for pickup.
  [7.1] Healthy Eating and Educational Approach to Food: Imagine Montessori School Valencia offers nutritionally balanced, daily scratch-made meals using organic, seasonal, local ingredients. We avoid processed foods (added fats/sugars), artificial colorings/preservatives, and keep salt low. Filtered water is available daily. Menus adapt to certified medical needs (gastroenterologist/allergist). Students eat together in classrooms, setting tables with ceramic plates/metal cutlery. Our Montessori-aligned gardening program teaches food origin, preparation, and waste management (compost, recycling), incorporating student-grown produce into lunch, fostering understanding of food-land links and seasonal cycles.
  [7.4] 7.4 Practical Matters: Students must bring a named toothbrush and toothpaste on day 1. Menus are shared monthly via Phidias. Notify administration before 09:30 for occasional soft diets or late arrivals needing special/adapted menus. Late adapted menu requests without prior notice cannot be guaranteed. A light snack is offered at 16:00; families should provide an additional snack for pickup.
[8] 8. Nap: Infant Community and Nursery (Casa de Niños) students have a rest period after lunch. The school provides a fitted sheet at the start of the year, including year-round laundry service. Parents provide a blanket when weather requires it. Costs are detailed in the fee document, found in the "Centre Documents" section on Phidias.
[9] 9. Health Standards: Parents must not send sick children to school. Children must stay home for 24 hours after the last fever (37.5ºC/99.5ºF), vomiting, or diarrhea. Other reasons to stay home include strep throat (24h on antibiotics), cold/flu, head lice (until treated), rash, or conjunctivitis (24h on antibiotics). For medication, complete the Phidias tracking form and provide signed authorization to the office/bus monitor. Only oral medications with a medical prescription and written family authorization are administered. Meds needed every 8+ hours should be given at home. If a child falls ill at school, immediate pickup is required (parent, then emergency contacts, or ambulance if critical).

Families must provide a medical certificate confirming adherence to the Valencian Community's vaccination schedule. This is vital for "herd immunity" and protecting the "immunocompromised." Certificates are required during Imagine admission and subsequently at ages 4, 6, 12, and 14.
  [9.1] 9.1 Medications and Illnesses: Do not send sick children to school. They must stay home for a minimum of 24 hours after the last fever (37.5ºC/99.5ºF or higher), vomiting, or diarrhea. Other reasons to stay home include strep throat (24h on antibiotics), cold/flu, head lice (until treated), rash, or conjunctivitis (24h on antibiotics). For medication, complete the Phidias tracking form. Hand meds to the office/bus monitor with signed authorization. We only administer oral medications with a medical prescription and written family authorization. Meds needed every 8+ hours should be given at home. If your child falls ill at school, we will contact you for immediate pickup, then emergency contacts, or call an ambulance if critical.
  [9.2] 9.2 Vaccination Status: Families must provide a medical certificate confirming adherence to the Valencian Community's vaccination schedule. This is crucial for student and community health, supporting "herd immunity" to protect the "immunocompromised." Certificates are required during the Imagine admission process, and subsequently at ages 4, 6, 12, and 14.
[10] 10. Behavior: For student behavior incidents during school hours, Imagine uses a referral form. Families are informed within 24 hours if a form is completed. After three incidents, a meeting is arranged with the family, teacher, and educational psychology team for early intervention to improve the situation and foster a positive school-home connection, ensuring a happy, safe environment.
[11] 11. Our Commitment to the English Language: As a British school, Imagine Montessori School Valencia's language of instruction is English. Enrolling signifies a conscious parental commitment to support English education. This includes encouraging English reading, prioritizing English speech over Spanish, using English music for listening/vocabulary, watching original English films/cartoons, and demonstrating interest by speaking or learning English.
[12] 12. Dress Code: Imagine does not require school uniforms, promoting student uniqueness. Optional garments are available via Phidias in the Imagine Shop. The Imagine sports shirt is mandatory for PE classes and excursions for safety/logistics. ALL clothing (socks, shoes, blankets, etc.) must be labelled with the student's name for easy identification; hanging items need long labels for self-hanging. Early Years clothing should be comfortable, practical, and easy to remove (e.g., elastic waistbands) to foster independence. Shoes should be easy for students to manage themselves.
[13] 13. Spare Clothing and Required Equipment: Families must provide labelled spare clothing: 2 sets for 2-3 year olds, 1 set for 4+. Replace if used. Students need closed-toe shoes/slippers for free play (if wearing sandals) and soft-soled shoes/indoor slippers for classroom/work patio. A maximum coverage hat is required for sunny seasons to prevent heatstroke/sunburn. Apply sun protection mornings; school reapplies. Ages 3+ practice self-application (roll-on helps).
[14] 14. Nappies and Accidents: Families must provide nappies & wipes for students using them in the Early Years Community. Children's House students are expected to be toilet-trained. For accidents, staff assist students to clean/change using family-provided spare clothes. Soiled items go home at pick-up; families must provide a new set to ensure two complete sets of spare clothes are always at school. A general change of clothes is also requested for unforeseen circumstances.
[15] 15. Birthday Celebrations (Early Years Community and Children's House): Birthday celebrations in Early Years/Children's House focus on recognizing growth and family involvement. Parents are asked to send "guide photos" (birth + one per year). Students can leave an enduring item for the classroom, such as a decorated vase or a library book. Due to potential allergies, there is a strict "no-foods policy"; the school provides usual snacks. We do not distribute party favors or invitations. Primary and Secondary teachers may modify celebration types.
[16] 16. Enrichment Programme: Our "Enrichment Programme" is an innovative extracurricular activities program for children and teenagers. It fosters creativity, social skills, and overall development through fun and learning, supported by highly trained professionals. Parents can enroll their child via the dedicated online process.
[17] 17. School’s Materials: If your child accidentally takes school educational material, please return the item to reception promptly. This is crucial because each piece belongs to a set, and incomplete sets become unusable. Your quick action ensures all learning resources remain functional.
[18] 18. Toys: Toys are generally not permitted at school. The only exception is a comfort item for naptime, but only if recommended by the guides. For Early Years children, pacifiers are permitted solely during naptime, if necessary, due to hygiene reasons and to aid language development.
[19] 19. Balls: Students may bring balls. For bus riders, balls must be kept in a bag, not loose, to prevent accidents. The school is not responsible for lost balls, including those that end up in the ditch. Balls are strictly for outdoor use, such as the playground. Misuse inside the premises will result in confiscation.
[20] 20. Lost and Found: The Lost and Found is located in a CH playground storage area. To help families recover misplaced items, all unclaimed belongings will be displayed monthly in the playground, typically on the last Friday afternoon. The exact day and time will be announced in advance. Please note: unclaimed items without a child's name will be cleared after this display day and may be donated or discarded. We strongly recommend labeling all personal belongings with your child’s name for easy return.
[21] 21. Outings: Outings complement learning, scheduled or ad-hoc, with potential additional costs communicated in advance. Children must participate as a group, starting and ending the day at school, using school-chosen transport for the full experience (e.g., Work Circle, behavioural rules). No personal booster seats are allowed on buses due to transport company policies. A specific consent form, signed via Phidias (not Secretary’s office), is mandatory for participation. Non-participating students attend school normally for same-day trips. Participation is ONLY with a signed consent form.
[22] 22. School Bus: The school bus service offers three routes, available for annual round-trip or one-way journeys. Routes may vary yearly, prioritizing continuity. This year, departure is 10 minutes later, but arrival times are unchanged due to 25/26 optimization.

Contracting the service occurs at academic year-end. For late requests, contact `admin@imaginemontessori.es` (subject to space).

Any changes (child not taking bus, exceptional stop change) must be communicated via a specific form *before 2:30 p.m.* No changes are accepted after this time or by phone/email. If picking up your child after 2:30 p.m., notify the secretary at school between 2:30 p.m. and 4:30 p.m. The bus departs at 4:50 p.m.
[23] 23. Updating Personal Information or Family Situation: Parents must update "contact information" or "important records" in Phidias immediately if they change, ensuring current data for emergencies. A video explains "How to edit your profile on Phidias." For changes in "family situation" (e.g., separation, divorce), inform your child's "guide" promptly so they can monitor any impact on the child.
[24] 24. Individual and Group Photographs: Imagine's policy requires all families to consent to sharing photos/videos of their children, taken by guides in shared spaces/teams to document progress, with other school families. For external media (press, website, social media), specific authorization is required; fewer images may be taken without it. The Annual Yearbook features professional individual/group photos of all children. Digital copies are provided at an additional cost, with advance notice. This digital format supports family preferences for various uses and aligns with sustainability principles.
[25] 25. Payment Policy and Financial Aid: Imagine's fees, updated annually, are paid monthly via direct debit. A flat monthly rate covers prorated costs, materials, and next course registration. Bus/activities are charged separately monthly. Proformas are sent by the 25th, direct debits by the 1st. Maria Luisa handles payment queries. Outings are charged monthly (Phidias authorization); timely notice is required for refunds. Enrichment is quarterly (first late Sept/early Oct). Next course enrollment is charged monthly Sept-June; refunded if non-continuity is communicated before Feb 1, otherwise charged until June. Returned direct debits incur a 2.5% bank commission.

Financial aid includes Valencia City Council (0-6 years) and Paterna City Council (0-3 years) School Vouchers, plus Sodexo nursery vouchers (Pluxe, UP Spain, Gourmet). Working mothers can claim a tax deduction via Form 233. Note: Lunch Aid, Generalitat Valenciana Childcare Voucher, and 0-3 Year Schooling Aid are NOT applicable due to Imagine's private, British curriculum status. Contact admin@imaginemontessori.es for financial aid questions.
  [25.1] 25.1 Payment Policy: Imagine's fees, updated annually on the website, are paid monthly by direct debit. A flat monthly rate covers prorated costs, materials, and next course registration. Bus/activities are charged separately monthly. Proformas are sent by the 25th, direct debits issued by the 1st. Contact Maria Luisa for questions. Outings are charged in the month they occur; Phidias authorization is used. Refunds for outings require timely notice. Enrichment is charged quarterly (first one late Sept/early Oct). Enrollment for the next course is charged monthly Sept-June; refunded if non-continuity is communicated before Feb 1, otherwise charged until June. Returned direct debits incur a 2.5% bank commission.
  [25.2] 25.2 Financial Aid: Families at Imagine can apply for Valencia City Council (0-6 years, May-April) and Paterna City Council (0-3 years, early May) School Vouchers. Sodexo nursery vouchers (Pluxe, UP Spain, Gourmet) are accepted. Working mothers can claim a tax deduction for schooling expenses via Form 233. Important: Lunch Aid, Generalitat Valenciana Childcare Voucher, and 0-3 Year Schooling Aid are NOT applicable to Imagine due to its private status and British curriculum. Contact admin@imaginemontessori.es for questions.
[26] 26. Comparison of the Spanish Education System vs the British System vs Montessori: Parents can compare British, Spanish, & Montessori education stages. The British system progresses from Early Years (Pre-Nursery, Nursery, Reception) to Key Stages 1-5 (Years 1-13). Spanish follows Educación Infantil (1º/2º ciclo e.i.), Primaria (1º-6º), ESO (1º-4º), then Bachiller (1º-2º). Montessori includes Infant Community, Children’s House, Lower/Upper Primary, and specific KS equivalents for adolescents. Ages 2-18 are covered.
[27] 27. School Policies: School policies are shared with families on Phidias, specifically within the "School Documents" section.

step2.sections_text:
[Section 7: 7. Nutrition]
## Healthy Eating and Educational Approach to Food

Nutrition is a central pillar in student development, and its impact extends far beyond health and well-being. It has a direct connection with our body, as well as with the science and biology that provides natural curricular links. At Imagine Montessori School Valencia, we provide meals made with high-quality ingredients. Our goal is to expose our students to a variety of tastes, textures, and aromas that contribute to a nutritious and healthy diet.

Our meals and snacks are nutritionally balanced and are made daily from scratch using organic, seasonal, and locally-sourced ingredients whenever possible. We do not use processed foods with added fats or sugars, and all meals are free from artificial colourings and preservatives, and are low in salt. The water we use is filtered and available in the classrooms every day, reducing the need for bottled water and eliminating the use of plastics.

We adapt our dishes to medical needs certified by a specialist (gastroenterologist and allergist) and offer a variety of menus to accommodate the needs of our community. All our food is prepared daily on-site by our chefs at Imagine, with passion for nourishing the bodies and minds of our students and staff.

Students eat together in their classrooms, and everyone helps before and after the meal. Students set the table daily using ceramic plates and metal cutlery.

We believe each student should have the opportunity to understand firsthand where their food comes from, how it is prepared, and how waste can be used productively. With this in mind, students take care of our gardens, manage our compost bin, and collect recyclable materials throughout the year. Depending on the season, vegetables, herbs, or fruits that students have planted, tended, and harvested are prepared and incorporated into the day's lunch.

Our gardening programme aligns closely with Montessori philosophy, offering real experiences with seeds, soil, water, and sunlight, as well as the life experience involved in growing food. As a result, students experience the process of where their food comes from and build a natural understanding of how food is linked to the land, its seasonal cycles, and the care required.

## 7.4 Practical Matters

After meals, students brush their teeth. Each student should bring a toothbrush and toothpaste with their name on it on the first day of school.

We share the menus with families monthly through Phidias. As attendance is taken and sent to the kitchen at 09:30, it is important **to inform the administration before this time** of the following situations:

*   Students who need a soft diet occasionally.
*   Students who arrive late and have a special diet.

For students with adapted menus who arrive late without prior notice, we cannot guarantee menu adjustments for that day.

Although we offer a light snack at 16:00, we recommend that families provide an additional snack for pickup.
